narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your plan to attend full-time like the vast majority of students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize applicants with stronger projected outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average around 5.7, reinforcing concerns about your early grades relative to successful bar takers. Your LSAT score of 30.5, lower than the average 36.7, played a notable role too, though it somewhat softened the prediction by pulling in the opposite direction toward passing, unlike your deciles which strongly amplified the risk. Overall, these academic rankings outweighed positives like your GPA and middle-family-income background, as the model weighs them heavily against historical patterns of bar success. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in higher deciles and score better on the LSAT, your profile aligned more closely with those who struggled on the exam. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income family—many in similar situations have succeeded with targeted improvements—but the model flagged these gaps as the biggest hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, while strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile rankings. You might also seek feedback from pre-law advisors on bolstering weaker years, as even modest gains there could shift future predictions dramatically. With persistence, revisiting your application next cycle could yield a different outcome, as the model responds well to upward trends in these key areas.
